text.title: api-monitoring-logging Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for api-monitoring-logging. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every HTTP request log entry MUST include: request_id (UUID, generated at request start), method, path, client_ip, response status, duration_ms, trace_id, span_id. The request_id MUST also be returned to the client as the X-Request-ID response header.
rule.rationale: The X-Request-ID header lets clients report the exact identifier when filing a bug report, enabling instant log lookup without timestamp guessing. trace_id joins the log line to the distributed trace for the same request.

rule.statement: Logs MUST NEVER contain passwords, tokens, API keys, credit card numbers (PAN), or session secrets. A redaction middleware MUST run before any log export. The redaction MUST cover both request bodies and structured log fields.
rule.rationale: AI agents wire request logging by calling logger.info(request.json()) — the entire request body, including credentials, goes into the log pipeline. Without a redaction layer, secrets leak into log aggregation platforms and their retention windows. A password-scrubber agent should run before logs are exported.

rule.statement: Async tasks (Celery workers, queue consumers, background jobs) MUST propagate OpenTelemetry trace context from the producer to the consumer. Use W3C traceparent header injection at the producer and extraction at the consumer. Without this, distributed traces appear as disconnected fragments.
rule.rationale: Sampling hides rare failures but trace context propagation gaps make debugging async work completely blind. A trace showing a request triggering a queue message, then silence, provides no insight into what the worker did. Context propagation turns disconnected fragments into end-to-end traces.
